data.title: CAVA Group: Unsupportable Valuation
data.description:
With the S&P 500 sitting at 20%+ gains for the year despite rumblings of potential macro malaise and a contentious U.S. election, investors have to take substantial care to ensure our portfolios are protected in the case of downside. Stock selection remains of utmost importance to beating the market averages in a time that I believe the S&P will trade sideways: and gains for me, this means pruning my holdings of overvalued and overhyped growth stocks and focusing on more value-oriented plays.

CAVA Group (NYSE:CAVA), in my view, is one of the stocks most at risk of a correction. The Mediterranean fast-casual chain, a rival of Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) that has used its successful IPO as both marketing momentum as well as capital to finance store expansions, has seen its share price triple year to date. The question for investors now is: can this rally keep going?

In my view, the clear answer is no. I last wrote a very bearish article on CAVA in July when the stock was trading at $85 per share. At the time, I had cited weak Q1 same-store sales growth and inflationary pressures as the core drivers for my pessimism. While my sell rating on the stock was certainly premature, and I didn’t expect the strong beat-and-raise in Q2 that featured a sharp re-acceleration in same-store sales, I am now even more concerned now about CAVA’s overinflated price relative to its fundamental realities. Though double-digit same store sales growth is reassuring, I’m still at a sell rating for CAVA ahead of earnings (expected on November 12), though my pessimism is slightly more tempered relative to my strong sell rating from earlier.

Valuation checkup

The crux of my concern on CAVA is price, so we’ll start there. At current share prices near $136, the stock trades at a massive market cap of $15.58 billion. And after we net off the $343.7 million of cash on the company’s latest balance sheet, its resulting enterprise value is $15.24 billion.

Yes, it’s true that CAVA raised its expectations for the current year across the board. Strong results in Q2 (which we’ll cover in more depth in the next section) prompted the company to raise its same-store sales growth expectations by 350bps at the midpoint, which funneled into higher restaurant-level operating margins and adjusted EBITDA as well. But we have to ask: is all of this already factored into CAVA’s share price? In my view, abundantly so.

Against the $111.5 million midpoint of the company’s latest guidance range, CAVA already trades at an enormous 137x adjusted EBITDA.

Even if we saw evidence of faster same-store sales growth (vs. the high single digits expected for the full year) and much faster adjusted EBITDA margin expansion, it’s hard to justify an adjusted EBITDA multiple above ~30-40x for this stock. Here’s how CAVA’s forward 12-month multiple compares against other fast-casual peers like Chipotle, Sweetgreen (SG), and Portillo’s (PTLO):

On top of valuation risk, I think CAVA is subject to quite a bit of fundamental risk as well. We should note that CAVA, especially with its latest marketing focusing on premium proteins like grilled steak, isn’t exactly positioned as a value meal out. Amid weaker consumer spending, we should consider the risk of weaker traffic, along with a number of other red flags:

Steer clear here and take advantage of the recent upside in CAVA stock to harvest gains on this stock.

Q2 download and Q3 look-ahead

This all being said, we should acknowledge that CAVA produced tremendous results in Q2. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Total revenue grew 35% y/y to $231.4 million, which accelerated versus just 30% growth in Q2. The bulk of the growth was due to the expanded restaurant portfolio, with the company opening 18 net-new restaurants in the quarter and with a restaurant count that was up 22% y/y.

But even more impressive was the fact that same-store sales growth (which measures growth from existing restaurants and excludes the contribution from newly added locations) jumped to 14.4%, the best showing in over a year and accelerating twelve points versus just 2.3% same-store sales growth in Q1, which had been concerning. We note that the company’s same-store sales growth compares favorably to many fast-casual peers like Chipotle, which posted high-single digit results in the same period.

Management noted that seasonal new menu releases, especially grilled steak which debuted at the start of July, had a positive impact on drawing in traffic. It also noted a very successful launch in Chicago. Per CEO Brett Schulman’s remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Additional highlights include our expansion into Chicago, which has been our strongest new market entry ever. We now have locations in Wicker Park and Vernon Hills, with a third slated to open an Oak Park next month.

During the second quarter, we also launched grilled steak, which is far surpassing our expectations. We were confident in steak's potential that since the national rollout, sales have been significantly higher than we saw in our seven-month market test. This new main protein complements our existing offerings, fills a perceived gap on our menu and is giving guests one more reason to visit CAVA and come back more often. The successive steak demonstrates our authority in culinary innovation and our ability to execute and I want to thank our cross-functional teams that work to bring this successful launch to life.”

Improved economies of scale, primarily on corporate overhead costs across the larger restaurant portfolio, as well as better-than-expected comp sales helped CAVA boost its adjusted EBITDA by 59% y/y to $34.3 million, representing a 14.7% margin, 220bps higher than 12.5% in the year-ago quarter.

Shifting ahead now to Q3: the company is expected to post Q3 results on November 12, which is the next major catalyst for the stock. In my view, the core items to watch out for are:

  • Will same-store sales hold up, or was Q2 a fluke? Strong mid-teens comp sales growth was the reason that CAVA soared post-Q2. It’s worth noting that the company used pro forma compares in Q2 to report non-GAAP measures, like comp sales, to get around the impacts of the company’s 53-week fiscal year in FY23. It did so by comparing a 12-week period this year versus similar dates in July last year. While the justification for the adjustments is certainly quite fair, we always have to watch out for the sustainability of strong performance when part of it is due to adjusted compares.
  • Will macro headwinds play any role? Of the company’s 14.4% same-store sales growth in Q2, about two-thirds was due to traffic growth and the rest was from menu pricing and product mix. As consumer spending continues to weaken, we’ll have to watch if traffic is impacted as more customers choose to eat at home, or if they start trading down to cheaper options even when eating out.
  • Is CAVA capable of meaningful FCF expansion? The company plans to open 54-57 stores this year and presumably keep a similar pace next year and beyond, with pre-opening costs of $12-13 million per location. The company only has about $340 million of cash remaining, meaning it’s relying at least in part on FCF (or another capital raise) to sustain its ambitions. It generated roughly $27 million of FCF in the first half of FY24, which means even including its profits on top of its existing balance sheet, it doesn’t have enough funds to support 50+ store openings over the next twelve months at its current FCF run rate.

Key takeaways

In my view, CAVA is laden with both fundamental risk from potentially slower consumer spending and tremendous valuation risk from its >100x adjusted EBITDA. Even if the company doubled its revenue base and doubled its adjusted EBITDA margins (quadrupling profits, effectively), it would only reach valuation levels that are simply similar to peers like Chipotle - and in my view, it’ll take a while for CAVA to even reach those levels, especially with the fast-casual market so laden with different choices for consumers.

Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Greek Mythology Reveals More About Oracle Than 10-Ks And Analyst Calls
data.description:
We’re all friends here. Right?

So, we can speak truthfully. None of us will judge anyone else.

Now… Did you snooze your way through literature courses you took in school?

I’ll start.

I confess that in my first semester after having transferred to Ohio State, I thought Comparative Literature sounded like an interesting elective. Why not? I’ve like fiction. I never went into the New York subway without a paperback novel in tow.

But oh my goodness: Book 2 of Homer’s classic The Iliad. The catalogue of ships! I couldn’t get through that. I wound up dropping the Comp Lit course.

Later on, as an adult, I tried again. I liked it so much, I read it several times. (It made a big difference knowing I could skim Book 2. There’d be no exam!)

I tackled much more over the years. And that includes a classic poet more remote than Homer. I’m referring to Hesiod. His Theogony is wild.

Many today argue the humanities can benefit in life… even in business.

I agree. Hesiod’s Theogony is helping me right now make sense of what I’m seeing with Oracle (NYSE:ORCL).

It reveals much more than SEC 10K and 10Q documents. And thanks to Theogony, I now recognize why Oracle’s investor communications are as they are.

Oracle and Cronus… Two of a Kind?

Even if you’re not into Greek mythology, I’ll bet you heard of Zeus. He was the father of the Greek Gods about which many myths deal. (The Romans later renamed these Gods. Zeus became Jupiter.)

But Greek myth doesn’t resemble Western religion. Zeus, even as King of the Gods, didn’t create the world.

He was actually third generation. Everything started with Father Sky (Uranus) and Mother Earth (Gaia). They had a bunch of children. Cronus (also called Kronos; the Romans called him Saturn) was the youngest of these “Titans.”

For reasons nastier and bloodier than need be explained here, Cronus overthrew his dad. Cronus and Rea, his Queen (and sister) co-ruled. That became known as the Golden Age.

But a prophecy made Cronus realize karma, when it would be invented later on, would be a nasty you-know-what. He came to fear that one of his children would overthrow him. It would be like what he did to dad.

Cronus had a solution. He ate each child just after it was born. So there’d be nobody to overthrow him.

Eventually, Rea cheated him out of one morsel. She wrapped a rock in cloth and served that to Cronus instead of the next born baby … Zeus.

She hid Zeus. He grew up. He eventually slipped Cronus a something that made him vomit up his other children. Zeus then led his siblings in a big war.

Zeus and the siblings won. They toppled Cronus.

So, Cronus was right to fear his children! Too bad for him that he failed to fully digest all of them.

Use this as context for what I’ll discuss about ORCL. Consider how obsessed ORCL is with its offspring, Snowflake (SNOW).

My 10/20/24 SNOW writeup paints a reactionary parent-rebellious child relationship between the two companies.

Snowflake is Living Rent Free in Oracle’s Corporate Head

I’ve been analyzing and writing about stocks since December 1979. (That’s when I started at Value Line.)

So, needless to say, I’ve seen a lot of stuff. And I’ve encountered many instances of company communications with the world.

But I’ve never seen anything remotely close to these three pages on Oracle’s corporate web site.

  • Five Reasons Why Autonomous Database is Better than Snowflake
  • Heat Wave vs. Snowflake: Top Advantages
  • Unlock your business potential with Oracle Autonomous Database

There may be more such content on the site. (Actually the first web page had much more than five things to say. It had five headings and recited multiple “reasons” under each.) But I already got the message. So, I stopped searching.

I0-K filings normally include sections addressed to competition. Often, though these mention rivals in perfunctory ways.

In SEC filings, on web sites and in presentations, companies typically devote most verbiage to explaining their own strengths. References to rivals tend to be generalized. “We’re better than competitors because….”

Conference calls or other meetings are more likely to discuss rivals by name. That’s especially so during question-and-answer sessions.

But I absolutely cannot recall ever having seen any company openly bash a named rival as relentlessly and vigorously as ORCL does viz. SNOW.

For the record, SNOW’s investor communications follow the typical pattern.

The Competition section of its latest 10-K (page 16) doesn’t name Oracle. And a text search shows the word “Oracle” is likewise absent from the rest of the filing. Too, the Why Snowflake content on its web site just discusses its own offering. It doesn’t mention Oracle.

Does ORCL founder Larry Ellison regret not being in tune with his inner Cronus?

Does he regret not having (metaphorically) eaten SNOW’s founders, former veteran well-respected Oracle Data Architects, before they left ORCL?

The metaphorical sons wanted to pursue the cloud.

But back in 2008, Ellison famously ranted…

The interesting thing about cloud computing is that we've redefined cloud computing to include everything that we already do. I can't think of anything that isn't cloud computing with all of these announcements. The computer industry is the only industry that is more fashion-driven than women's fashion. Maybe I'm an idiot, but I have no idea what anyone is talking about. What is it? It's complete gibberish. It's insane. When is this idiocy going to stop?

ORCL stuck to Ellison’s guns. So, in 2012, ORCL’s former star architects founded SNOW.

It’s different now.

Today ORCL is in the cloud. They often refer to it as Autonomous Data Warehouse, or Autonomous Database, or ADW.

And besides bashing SNOW, ORCL is talking itself up aggressively.

Here, for example, are some of the slides from two presentation decks prepared for the company’s 9/12/24 Oracle Cloud World Financial Analyst Meeting.

(You can see both of the complete decks, and some conference videos here.)

I can accept ORCL’s promotion of its own cloud. What business doesn’t do that!

I suppose I could also learn to live with the way ORCL bashes SNOW. It’s not inconsistent with the world in which we now live. Look for example, at how nasty political debate rhetoric has become.

But something else still bothers me…

Oracle’s Presentation of Itself and Its Cloud Doesn’t Quite Jibe with What I’m Seeing from Independent Observers

For example, the Five Reasons web page referenced above slams SNOW saying it “doesn’t support automated in-database machine learning.”

ORCL claims it has a “full integrated AutoML user interface.”

Separately, ORCL charges that SNOW “users must rely on a separate service, Snowflake Cortex, to use external LLMs.” Meanwhile, ORCL supposedly can “integrate with new LLMs as they become available.”

Here’s what ORCL isn’t saying: Its ML, etc. features are no more native than SNOW’s. ORCL has been an on major acquisition spree to buy capabilities.

SNOW, too, has acquired.

But check out the relative debt and free cash flow comparisons. (These necessarily are reflect considerable differences in scale. ORCL’s TTM revenue is $53.82 billion. SNOW’s is $3.21 billion.)

That’s just a minuscule sample of things that bothered me about ORCL’s communications.

Ultimately, investment professionals such as I find it exceptionally challenging to assess pure product issues like cloud data offerings.

We may have a users’ vantage point. But our knowledge typically lacks professional depth.

There are a few things I definitely know. But for the most part, I’m a prisoner of whatever external commentary I found (and understood).

So, besides the ORCL sources to which I linked above, I Googled like crazy.

Here are the non-Oracle sources I found most comprehensible. (The world evolves. So I dated the sources and ordered them starting with the most recent.)

  1. Snowflake Vs Oracle - Key Differences (10/14/24)
  2. Oracle or Snowflake: Choosing the Best Database (6/26/24)
  3. Snowflake vs Oracle: 3 Critical Parameters (5/29/24)
  4. Snowﬂake vs. Oracle: Which Data Warehouse is Better? (2/19/24)
  5. Critical Capabilities for Cloud Database Management Systems for Operational Use Cases (1/8/24)
  6. Oracle Autonomous Data Warehouse vs Snowflake Data Cloud: A brief overview (5/17/23)
  7. Is Oracle's Database Dominance Being Eroded by Cloud-First Rivals? (6/13/22)
  8. Comparison of Snowflake vs. Oracle 2024: Which Data Warehouse is Best for You? (undated)

I also queried ChatGPT, and to lesser degrees, Google Gemini and Meta AI. I did so to see if I was missing things they were seeing. (I wasn’t.)

The first (freshest) 10/14/24 source summarizes much of what I saw in the others…

Oracle’s architecture suits transactional workloads (OLTP), while Snowflake is ideal for analytical processing (OLAP). Oracle supports hybrid environments, combining on- premise and cloud systems. Whereas Snowflake operates entirely in the cloud, using public cloud platforms for infrastructure. Both systems perform well, but Snowflake’s architecture is more tailored to cloud-native, data-intensive environments.

* * * *

Oracle's scalability can be more manual, especially in on-premise environments, as adding resources requires complex configuration. It offers better scalability in cloud setups, but manual intervention is still needed to fine-tune performance. Oracle’s performance tuning often involves adjustments to optimize queries and resource allocation.

In contrast, Snowflake automatically scales computing and storage based on demand without user involvement. This auto-scaling ensures consistent performance even

during peak workloads. Snowflake’s design eliminates the need for manual tuning, making it highly suitable for analytics tasks.

Oracle performs well for transactional systems but requires more effort to scale efficiently. Snowflake works better for seamless, real-time scaling, particularly for data- heavy analytical workloads, offering consistent performance with minimal intervention.

* * * *

If you're looking for a platform that's easy to set up and manage, Snowflake might be the better choice. Snowflake is known for its user-friendly interface and minimal maintenance requirements because of its fully managed cloud architecture. Oracle, while extremely powerful, often requires more technical expertise to set up and maintain, especially in on-premise or hybrid environments.

* * * *

If your business requires a combination of cloud and on-premise solutions, Oracle provides more flexibility. Oracle supports both cloud and traditional on-premise deployments, offering flexibility for hybrid deployments. However, if you're fully dependent on the cloud and don't need on-premise options, Snowflake is ideal as it offers seamless cloud-native functionality.

* * * *

Snowflake offers Secure Data Sharing, which allows you to easily share data with internal and external stakeholders without the need for data movement or duplication. Oracle, on the other hand, provides robust data sharing features but may require more manual effort than Snowflake's more streamlined approach.

* * * *

If cost flexibility is your top priority, Snowflake is likely the better option. Snowflake's usage-based pricing model allows you to only pay for the storage and computing resources you actually use. Oracle, while powerful, tends to be more expensive with its licensing and maintenance fees.

I’ll add that independent sources agree that both offerings are fine when it comes to privacy and security.

The sources, as exemplified by the one I extensively quoted, are balanced. Each offering has its own distinct merits. So, customers shouldn’t simply flip a coin. Many will be better served by ORCL. Many others should pick SNOW.

At any rate, the balanced sources are a far cry from many impressions one can glean from the ORCL web material. Aside from ML and the like, the ORCL site implies SNOW is much more expensive, and harder to set up and use.

ORCL is making much of its availability on all three major hyperscaler clouds (Amazon AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud). But that’s a wash. So, too, is SNOW.

What Investors Should Make of All This

As a company, ORCL is ok.

Its presentations recite some impressively large customer numbers. So, too, does the latest earnings call.

That’s no shock.

ORCL has been around since 1977. And as of 9/18/24, ORCL ranked 89th on the Fortune 500 list. (Its rank also put it in the Fortune 100.)

And as of June 2024, Oracle was number one Statista’s ranking of “popular relational database systems worldwide.”

(By way of contrast, SNOW, to which ORCL calls so much attention, is a still tiny firm that launched in 2012).

To me, it looks like ORCL is and will likely remain top dog in the traditional data world. Relational databases are a big deal. Here’s how I described them in my 10/20/24 SNOW article:

The relational element is what makes big data click. Simply (very simply) put, imagine a customer data set and also, a purchase order data set.

The company needs to track both. It would be unwieldy to put everything in one giant data table. So, they’re kept separate.

But one piece of information is common to both tables… a Customer ID. The “key” lets the tables relate to one another.

That makes it easy for the company to track customer behavior, customer accounts, inventory, and product popularity as a whole and based on region and customer demographics.

This sort of thing is incredibly entrenched. I assume much of the traditional business world would find it hard to eliminate relational data.

Since ORCL is the long-standing king here, I can’t imagine the company vanishing.

I can easily see ORCL’s cloud being very appealing to established enterprises that want to migrate such operations from on-premises to the cloud. The independent sources cited above concur.

The 9/10/24 Morningstar report (which you can probably access for free through your on-line broker) is unenthusiastic. It says…

[W]e don’ t view the company as being on the forefront of recent software trends, and new and potential customers appear to be looking past Oracle for their database needs. Database preferences are far wider today due to the sheer number of ways to manipulate data, and the different data storage practices this necessitates. In turn, Oracle is losing database market share to new database types that may be better suited to the cloud.

The 6/13/22 source I cited above was harsher:

Oracle makes a significant portion of its revenue on existing customers. Every few years, when companies have to renew their contracts, Oracle can raise prices for maintenance and support — a business with margins hovering around 95%, according to Craig Guarente, a 16-year veteran of Oracle who is now CEO and co-founder of consulting firm Palisade Compliance.

"The entire profit of the company comes from Oracle database maintenance," he said. With each contract negotiation, "you go from paying $20 million a year, to $30 million a year, to paying $50 million a year."

Guarente’s summary largely matches my own experience with other firms. During a large portion of my career, I was involved in negotiating contracts for financial data.

What he says about ORCL strikes me as plausible. It reminds me of how I felt about the data vendors with which I dealt.

I understand the quote comes from 2022. Things evolve over time. But if ORCL’s situation has improved, the company isn’t allowing us to see it.

Here is the company’s breakdown of segment revenues and operating profits.

That may be the single worst set of segment reports I’ve seen since I’ve been analyzing stocks.

The cloud, like almost everything else about the company, is buried in the absurdly titled and grotesquely inclusive “Cloud and License” segment.)

To my mind, all this paints ORCL as a very strong company. But also, I’m inclined to attach to it such labels as “mature” and “legacy.”

Snowflake envy here seems quite understandable. That, not ORCL, is the choice for those seeking to ride AI and other future-oriented trends.

Oracle’s Numbers

The company’s numbers are not special. Let’s call most of them OK.

Risks

A big risk is Oracle’s position in data. Will on-premises data, the company’s bailiwick, fully give way to the cloud? And where, exactly, does Oracle’s cloud stand relative to newer cloud-native rivals?

Rhetoric from outside Oracle suggests shareholders should worry.

But those sources aren’t necessarily comprehensive. And objectivity is always a question. (ORCL’s own unhelpful segment reporting doesn’t help.)

So market share questions remain open.

And I’m concerned about ORCL’s balance sheet and financial choices.

There’s a lot of debt here.

That said, ORCL doesn’t resemble a junk-bond issuer. A 4-plus interest coverage ratio does not come close to posing a failure risk.

But the Return on Equity scares me.

For quant geeks, yeah, it’s way the heck above cost of capital. But I don’t care.

I’m bothered by how high it is compared to Return on Assets. We see that when a company relies very heavily on debt capital.

High debt leverage is fine for mature stable businesses. It can be the lifeblood of private equity.

I don’t like it for a tech leader that needs to transition to a new cloud-oriented computing paradigm.

I don’t like seeing, as we did above, key financial indicators for ORCL being generally less than those of an emerging small cap, SNOW.

I suppose Cronus, observing from Mount Olympus or wherever mythic divinities hang out, sneering at ORCL: “You’re repeating my mistake. You should have swallowed the kid. And unlike me, you should have kept it down.”

What to do About ORCL Stock

I’m sure you recognize by now that I’m not an ORCL fan. You might expect me to say “Sell.”

Not so fast!

I believe in separating my opinion about a company from my opinion about its stock.

Many Seeking Alpha analysts do likewise. You see that often nowadays. Many articles are positive about companies but less so about stocks that rose too much.

I’m now coming at this from the opposite direction. You know I don’t like the company.

I'm less negative on the stock. Here, start with the basic valuation metrics.

I’m not overly impressed… favorably or unfavorably. I’m in the middle.

Here’s the stock chart.

The recently rising and now settling 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) remains well above the rising 50-day EMA.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF), focusing on institutional investors, measures which party to trades is more motivated. It’s positive now, suggesting a tailwind for the stock. But it seems to be a so-so wind.

The Chaikin Oscillator (CO) plays the same role for the market in general. It’s very modestly on the downside of neutral.

To me, this portrays a stock that lacks serious buying pressure at the moment. But sellers aren’t dominating either.

I put that in the context of the story. I don’t see ORCL as a tech leader. But its legacy businesses remain quite large. It’s no sin to be mature… even in tech.

As I’ve said before, my investment stance depends mainly on whether I think a stock will be better than, in line with, or worse than market.

Here’s how I apply that to the Seeking Alpha rating system:

  • “Strong Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market and I’m bullish about the direction of the market.
  • “Buy” means I see the stock as being better than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Hold” means I see the stock as moving in line with the market.
  • “Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market but am not confident about the market’s near-term direction.
  • “Strong Sell” means I see the stock as being worse than the market and I’m bearish about the direction of the market.

Based on this scale, I’m rating ORCL as a “Hold.

data.title: Simon Property Group: The Rebirth Of Malls Is Experiential
data.description:
Overview

I previously covered Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) back in June and issued a buy rating due to the stock remaining undervalued and the dividend being supported with a large cushion by the current earnings. Since that prior article, SPG has provided a total return that outpaces the S&P 500, accompanied by a recent dividend raise by a modest 2.5%. Simon Property Group operates as a real estate investment trust that generates its earnings from a portfolio of different properties, primarily consisting of malls and shopping outlets.

Ever since the start of the pandemic in 2020, I've commonly read that malls are dying. While I would agree that they do not generally hold the same value as they once did a generation ago, I believe that the landscape of what we consider a mall is changing. Therefore, we should change our perspective of these sorts of properties to really understand why there is still a ton of value to be captured from a REIT like SPG. Over the last decade, SPG has struggled to gain any positive price momentum, further delayed by the drop in 2020. However, I believe that SPG is one of those unique businesses that shouldn't be defined by its past performance, and we should instead look to the future.

The total return over the last decade has been supported by consistent dividends, with a yield now sitting around 4.7%. The dividend continues to be well-supported by the strong financials of SPG. I believe that the dividend growth will become more attractive over time, making SPG a great choice for investors that also value a reliable stream of income from REIT exposure. I wanted to first start this off by taking a look into the current portfolio strength of the REIT and then dive into some thoughts of the changing landscape of mall properties and how SPG will likely capitalize on these shifts.

Portfolio Strength & Financials

Part of SPG's strength comes from its diverse portfolio of properties that are spread across the US, as well as a sizeable amount of international exposure. The latest supplemental data confirms that Florida is their biggest market, accounting for 19.1% of net operating income. This is followed by exposure to the California and Texas markets, accounting for 13.9% and 10.2% respectively. We can also see that US-based malls and outlets are what make up 71.6% of their earnings power.

The revenue streams are so large for Simon that they break off their business into four separate segments. The US malls and premium outlets are the largest sources of revenue. The following segment is referred to as 'The Mills', which are typically value oriented outlets that shoppers can visit to find more attractive sales. The international segment is self-explanatory and consists of properties that are mainly within different regions of Europe and Asia. The last segment is referred to as 'TRG', which represents the acquisition of the Taubman Realty Group that was completed back in 2020.

SPG reported their Q2 earnings back at the start of August and the results were solid. FFO per share came in at $2.90 while revenue landed at $1.46B for the quarter. The level of earnings continues to be strong, driven by the consistent occupancy levels that have trended upward since the pandemic. Occupancy at the end of June sat at 95.6%, representing an increase of 0.9% year over year. As occupancy increases, SPG has been able to capitalize on a growing base minimum rent per square foot of $57.94. This is a 3% increase from the year prior base minimum rent per square foot of $56.27.

A rising occupancy level isn't only great for total earnings potential, but also serves as an indicator that there is a sizeable amount of demand still for these retail spaces. Not to mention, SPG has been able to achieve this growing occupancy during a time of elevated interest rates and higher inflation levels that has negatively impacted consumer spending levels.

Speaking of higher interest rates, SPG currently has debt totaling $23.2B. While this is an admittedly large amount of debt, the total debt burden has modestly improved from the $24.9B in long-term debt that was owed at the end of 2022. As debt decreases, the cash position has increased and strengthened the overall quality of SPG's balance sheet. SPG current has $1.23B in cash and equivalents that can help them navigate future headwinds, reinvest back into the growth of their business, or go towards dividends and share repurchases. Thankfully, SPG's credit ratings are strong and pose no current vulnerability to the financial health.

However, I do remain a bit cautious as I believe that the top tenants can be a source of weakness. For instance, The Gap (GAP) is their largest tenant and accounts for 2.8% of SPG's annual base rent. GAP has strong financials at the moment with a healthy balance of cash and debt, but have a growing list of store closures since 2020, now sitting over 350 locations. Similarly, Signet Jewelers Limited (SIG) plans to close over 150 locations.

However, I don't mention these two businesses by name because I think they are inherently weak companies. I mention these names because they all share a similar path of seeing an increase percentage of their sales coming from online sources. At some point, we have to question the longevity of these physical locations and whether or not they are actually a requirement in this modern era of consumer spending.

A lot of these concerns are outweighed by the fact that management continues to allocate new dollars towards different growth initiatives such as new development projects. For instance, SPG committed $1.12B towards new developments and redevelopment projects through FY25, which is expected to generate a stabilized return of 8%. Redevelopments within the mall segment accounted for $868M, making up the largest bulk of their investment. These new developments can help SPG capitalize on a changing retail landscape, further strengthened by the recent announcement of a $3.5B revolving credit facility.

Changing Landscape

It isn't shocking news that the habits of consumers are changing. As expected, online sales have consistently grown to account for a larger percentage of total retail sales over the last few years. Estimates by Insider Intelligence indicate that the share of ecommerce sales are expected to grow to 20.6% of total retail sales by the end of 2027, up from the estimated 16.6% for the end of 2024. As a larger percentage of sales shift to online sources, I believe that SPG will shift their tenant exposure and account for these changes.

More specifically, I believe that malls will slowly shift to include a lot more experiential based tenants over time to offset this vulnerability. This would include embracing certain experienced based businesses like movie theaters, bowling alleys, a greater emphasis on dining experiences, and other sort of recreational based tenants. If you are a frequent mall-goer, you've likely already noticed this shift in some of the popular malls in your area.

Although not related to SPG specifically, a real-world example of this sort of experiential focus would be the local American Dream mall based in NJ. This mall aims to offer a one of a kind experience with the inclusion of an amusement park, mini-golf park, indoor waterpark, and an actual indoor ski slope. Times are changing and there is plenty of data that suggests millennials and Gen Z prefer to spend their money on experiences as opposed to material items. A recent example of SPG taking part of this change would be the deal they struck with 'Camp' to open two new locations that serve to attract families looking to have fulfilling experiences.

A retail forecast compiled by Retail Strategies seems to agree with this exact assessment on the outlook of malls. We can see that they estimate the strongest categories for growth comes from and experiential sectors. In this scenario, it includes experiences such as eGaming, virtual reality, culture exhibits, and other competitive socializing. The competitive socializing area would include finding tenants that support spaces like gyms for basketball games, courts for pickleball or tennis, and creative businesses like escape rooms.

The most recent example of SPG implementing some of these changes would be from their main site where they have a breakdown of different experiences they are currently looking to expand. However, I look forward to announcements of new developments in the experience space over FY25. Failure to adapt to these experiential based tenants can be a source of vulnerability.

Risk Profile

If SPG fails to adapt to changing consumer habits, earnings will be negatively impacted over time. This is assuming that some of the retail tenants start to close locations and shift their business models to incorporate a greater online presence. Empty stores would essentially hurt their bottom line for annual base rent collection and SPG may have to offer lower rents to attract new tenants to replace these tenants. This could cause occupancy levels to decrease over time, which may also lower investor confidence.

A great example of a peer REIT that comes to mind would be EPR Properties (EPR), which has a portfolio of different experiential based tenants. While this REIT's structure and strategy isn't necessarily mall-based like SPG's I think their list of tenants would be a good starting point that SPG could use as a reference. EPR has exposure to experienced based tenants such as movie theaters, eat & play tenants that offer a gaming and food experience, as well as amusement park attractions. If SPG fails to adapt and get an edge in this area, investor capital could transition into peers like EPR that are better suited to capitalize on the shift.

Another potential risk could be a possible change in management sometime down the line. It was announced earlier in the year that the CEO, David Simon, was undergoing cancer treatment. David will still serve as CEO of the business for the time being, and his son has been elected to serve as a Senior Vice President of Corporate Investments. While I certainly wish David a speedy recovery, the possibility of a change in management could be something we see happening eventually.

Dividend

As of the latest declared quarterly dividend of $2.05 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 4.7%. As a divided investor, I really value that SPG's dividend coverage currently sits at a rate that allows me to sleep well at night. As previously mentioned, FFO came in at $2.90 per share over the last quarter. This means that the earnings cover the dividend at a comfortable rate of 141.4%, which means that SPG can continue to be a core holding that continually compounds my annual dividend income with each passing quarter. In addition, this strong coverage allows there to be some optimism when it comes to future dividend growth.

It's also reassuring that management reinforced this positive outlook on a recent global real estate conference with Bank of America, by confirming that free cash flow levels are strong even after distributing dividend payments out to shareholders.

Net debt to EBITDA is about 5.2 times. The balance sheet is well poised to continue to support our growth going forward. The company generates about $1.5 billion of free cash flow after paying its dividend annually that's available for reinvestment in its properties, which is a rather unique situation relative to other REITs in the world. - Brian McDade, Chief Financial Officer

While the dividend did previously get reduced back in 2020, this was driven by the weakness of lower sales volumes at their locations as a result of the lockdowns during the pandemic. Therefore, I do not hold this cut against management and fully understand the reasoning to do so in an effort to preserve value. Since then, the dividend growth has been meaningful, considering that the yield is already sufficient. For instance, the dividend has increased at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 5.13% over the last five-year period.

The divided was cut to a quarterly rate of $1.30 per share during the pandemic in 2020. This has since increased to a quarterly total of $2.05 per share, representing an increase of 57.7%. If SPG continues to grow at an attractive rate like this, it will improve investor yield on cost. This adds to the appeal of using SPG as an income compounding vehicle. Since the coverage rate is so strong, this creates a 'sleep well at night' appeal where investors do not have to be concerned on whether or not any headwinds will reduce the income generated from this position.

However, it's worth noting that the distributions received from SPG are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have less favorable tax consequences than the qualified dividends that you'd receive from more traditional dividend paying stocks. Based on your own situation, it may be worth it to considering placing an SPG position inside of a tax advantaged account to offset this tax burden.

Valuation Outlook

SPG currently trades at a price to AFFO ratio of 14.43x, undercutting the sector median price to AFFO ratio of 15.64x. This may indicate that SPG is trading at a discount to its peers and is an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares. Wall St. seems to be a bit more pessimistic with their outlook based on the average price target of $167.71 per share, which represents a slight downside potential of about 4% from the current price level. However, I remain a bit more optimistic with my outlook because of the favorable interest rate environment ahead.

As previously mentioned, SPG currently holds $24.9B of long-term debt on their balance sheet. This debt is held at a weighted average interest rate of 3.75% and the majority of this debt has a maturity runway between six or seven years. As SPG relies heavily on debt to finance new developments and other growth initiatives, future interest rate cuts will provide some relief for any of this debt that has been taken on a floating rate basis.

The Fed has already implemented their first interest rate cut of 50 basis points in mid-September. Optimism and anticipation around interest rate cuts have boosted the real estate sector up a bit, and I expect there to be additional rate cuts to follow. Lower interest rates directly translate to a lower cost of debt for a business like SPG, so I believe cuts to serve as a strong positive catalyst for growth over the next twelve months.

Recently economic data such as an upward trending unemployment rate could contribute to the likelihood of another rate cut. The unemployment rate now sits at 4.1% and as more households are out of work, consumer spending levels may temporarily drop or become more geared towards SPG's 'Mill' segment where shoppers can find a greater variety of deals. At the same time, inflation levels have consistently trended downward of the last six months and can provide another group of consumers some relief.

Inflation now sits at 2.4% and, while remaining higher than the Fed's 2% target, serves as a sign of relief for consumers. As some of the higher income consumers see relief, this can simultaneously boost the traffic levels that the higher-end luxury tenants see. It especially helps that we are now in Q4 and heading towards the holiday spending season. The combination of these factors can be enough to incentivize the Fed to begin reducing interest rates.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating for Simon Property Group as occupancy levels are strong and support a healthy FFO level. Earnings currently support the dividend with a wide margin of safety and lead to an increased likelihood of future dividend rises. The portfolio of properties remains diverse in nature and I believe that SPG has an opportunity to continue its growing momentum by shifting a focus towards experiential based properties. Data supports the notion that Millennials and Gen Z would prefer to spend their money on experiences rather than material items and SPG can use this as an opportunity for growth. I believe that the valuation still remains attractive since the price to AFFO ratio remains under the sector median and interest rate cuts can serve as a positive price catalyst for growth.

data.title: Ally Financial: Shares Are Unlikely To Rebound Until Delinquencies Stabilize
data.description:
While shares of Ally Financial (NYSE:ALLY) have been a strong performer over the past year, up 48%, their recent performance has been poor as investor concern about rising credit costs intensifies. Q3 earnings results have done little to assuage these fears, given higher charge-offs. I last covered Ally in July, downgrading shares from a “buy” to “hold,” but with the stock down 20%, a downgrade all the way to “sell” would have been justified. With new financials and shares having underperformed, now is a good time to revisit ALLY.

In the company’s third quarter, Ally earned $0.93 in adjusted EPS, which actually beat consensus by $0.43. However, there were $0.57 of tax benefits, which likely made its reported EPS not comparable to consensus. Simply put, this quarter was not materially better than expectations, as the company faces higher credit costs and disappointing interest margins.

First, as I discussed in July, there is some accounting noise around tax incentives for electric vehicle (EV) leases that reduce Ally’s tax bill today, inflating earnings. These savings are then passed on to consumers over the course of the lease, so it is entirely a timing issue. In Q3, there were $179 million in EV lease tax credits. $107 million was related to Q3 volumes, and there was a $72 million true-up from H1 activity. Because it books the tax gain first, it then records less interest income, a 6bp headwind to reported net interest margin (NIM). Because of this accounting treatment, Ally had a -53% adjusted tax rate, which speaks to how much EVs are distorting it given a corporate tax rate of 21%.

Alongside results, Ally said it was considering changing its accounting method to no longer recognize the tax gain all at once, instead deferring it over the life of the loan. This does not change the life of the loan’s profitability, but it will reduce book value by $309 million, as any change will be retroactive. While I believe this change would improve the comparability of earnings, there would be a 20bp capital hit, given the $309 million reversal.

Moving away from accounting details and to actual results, ALLY continues to gradually shrink its loan book. Total loans and leases fell $900 million to $146.2 billion, with yields down 3bps to 8.19%. Risk weighted assets are down $5 billion over the past year, helping to drive a 50bp increase in its common equity tier 1 capital ratio (CET1).

Because of shrinking loans, ALLY can allow some of its higher-cost deposits to mature. On an end of period basis, deposits fell by $600 million, given a smaller loan book. In Q3, it had $152.2 billion in average deposits, down 0.1% sequentially and about $1.3 billion from last year. Management has actively let deposits decline, given a drop in loans.

Given Ally’s strong history of deposit accumulation, I am comfortable with this modest pullback, especially as loan growth is likely to be muted in the current consumer credit environment. Plus, even with slightly lower deposits, Ally’s liquidity position has continued to improve as it has a 96% loan to deposit ratio from 98% last year.

While its trend in deposits makes sense, I was disappointed that the average cost rose 2bps to 4.23%. I would have hoped to see a more meaningful improvement from the Fed rate cut. Because deposit costs have proven stickier, Ally reported a 3.25% net interest margin, down 1bp from last year and down 5bps sequentially.

Now, Ally did cut deposit rates 20bps in September after the Fed rate cut, but this likely occurred too late in the quarter to impact results. Still, this was just 40% of the Fed’s move, whereas Ally aims to pass 70% on to customers. Consequently, Ally sees NIM “choppy” based on competitive trends for deposits and expects “slow” deposit betas in the near-term. Combined with $65 billion of floating rate asset exposure, this has pressured NIM, leading Ally to cut 2024 guidance, even as it still sees 4% over the medium term.

Beyond rate pressures, credit has been a focus, playing a larger role in the share price decline over the past two months. Ally took $645 million in provisions for credit losses, up $137 million from last year. This was also up from $457 million in Q2. Charge-offs in Q3 underperformed expectations, as the benefit from vintage shift has not been as large as hoped. Indeed, they rose 24bps sequentially to 1.5%.

For much of the past year, I have written about credit normalization, with losses worsening from extremely low levels but eventually settling out. To this point, we are seeing the pace of worsening decelerate. Charge-offs are up 39bps YoY from a peak of 118bps. However, the fact that they are still rising, alongside delinquencies, is disappointing.

As you can see below, delinquencies rose 18bps to 4.51%. Most concerningly, 90+ day delinquencies rose 8bps to 0.59%. These are the delinquencies most likely to result in defaults and charge-offs. That continued rise likely means credit costs will stay higher for longer.

Because of worse credit performance, Ally has added to reserves. It now has 3.8% coverage of auto reserves to auto loans, up 15bps, due to Hurricane Helene-related losses and a slower improvement in charge offs. $3.2 billion of its $3.7 billion in reserves are for its auto lending business. Indeed, if there was one bright spot, its smaller credit card division saw improvement with charge-offs down 270bps to 9.9%.

Ally has taken action to tighten credit standards. Over the past two years, its auto loan approval rate has declined from 35% to 28% with an average FICO score of 710, up 22 points. Its borrower’s average payment to income ratio is down 1.4% to 8.3%. As such, credit costs should improve as it moves past its 2022 vintage. As you can see below, credit trends are better for 2023 loans than 2022 with 2024 better than 2023 at this point. While the improvement has been incremental, these shifts do support a peak in credit costs, likely sometime next year, about a year later than I initially expected.

Importantly, Ally does remain well capitalized, with a 9.8% CET1 capital ratio. This is above its long-term 9-9.5% target. However, with the EV accounting change likely to be a 0.2% hit and a 19bp impact to capital in Q1 from CECL accounting phase-in, capital is a pro forma 9.4%. This is still solid, but I do not expect capital returns outside its 3.5% dividend until 2026 at the soonest.

As you can see below, Ally has adjusted its expectations for 2024. Full-year guidance is for a 10bp lower NIM and 5bp higher charge-off rate, with retail auto charge-offs up 15-20bps. Essentially, it is making a bit less on rates and facing more credit costs. This double impact has been a major headwind for shares.

With NIM likely to run about 10bps narrower than I previously expected and credit costs taking longer to normalize, I am looking for $3.50-$4.00 in EPS over the next year from $4-$4.50. I have viewed 11x as a full forward multiple given its higher-risk loan book, but with delinquencies still rising, shares may stay closer to 10x. ALLY has a $36.43 adjusted tangible book value, and I view 1-1.1x as a fair valuation until we see delinquencies stabilize.

These various valuation metrics each point to about $35-37.50 in as a fair value, or 2-8% of upside. In other words, I do think the recent sell-off has been largely justified by fundamentals, and risks are relatively well priced. However, until we see credit trends stabilize, shares are likely to have limited upside. If shares fell toward $33 or 10% below book value, I would likely move to a buy. But for now, I remain a hold given limited upside and disappointing recent operating performance.
